items.question: She ___ to work every day.
explanations: 'Walk' is the base form for I/you/we/they. With he/she/it the present simple adds -s.
Correct! Third person singular (she) takes -s in the present simple: 'walks'.
'Is walking' is present continuous — for right now. A daily routine takes the present simple: 'walks'.
'Walking' on its own is not a full verb here. The habit needs 'walks'.

items.question: ___ your brother ___ English?
explanations: Correct! Questions use Do/Does + subject + base verb. With 'your brother' (he) it's 'Does he speak?'.
After 'does' the main verb goes back to its base form — 'speak', not 'speaks'. The -s is already in 'does'.
'Do' doesn't agree with 'your brother' (he/she/it), which needs 'does'.
'Is … speak' mixes two structures. A present simple question is 'Does your brother speak…?'

items.question: We ___ on Sundays.
explanations: After 'don't' the verb is the base form: 'work', not 'works'.
'Doesn't' is for he/she/it. 'We' takes 'don't'.
Correct! Negatives use don't/doesn't + base verb. With 'we' it's 'We don't work'.
English needs an auxiliary in the negative: 'do not (don't) work', not just 'not work'.

items.question: Water ___ at 100°C.
explanations: 'Is boiling' describes water boiling right now. A scientific fact that is always true takes the present simple.
Correct! General truths and facts take the present simple: 'Water boils at 100°C'.
'Water' is like 'it', so the verb needs -s: 'boils'.
'Boiled' is past simple — but a permanent fact is true now and always, so use the present simple.

items.question: He ___ coffee. He prefers tea.
explanations: Correct! He/she/it uses 'doesn't' + base verb: 'He doesn't drink coffee'.
'Don't' is for I/you/we/they. 'He' takes 'doesn't'.
After 'doesn't' the verb is the base form — 'drink', not 'drinks'.
'Isn't drinking' means not drinking right now. A general habit takes the present simple: 'doesn't drink'.

items.question: I ___ get up early on Saturdays.
explanations: Correct! Adverbs of frequency like 'never' go before the main verb: 'I never get up early'.
'Am' isn't needed with an ordinary verb. Only the verb 'be' takes this pattern (I am never late).
The word order is wrong. Frequency adverbs come before the main verb: 'I never get up'.
'Never' is already negative, so 'don't never' is a double negative. Use 'I never get up'.

items.question: The train ___ at 6 o'clock tomorrow morning.
explanations: For a fixed timetable, English prefers the present simple even about the future: 'The train leaves at 6'.
Correct! Timetables and schedules use the present simple: 'The train leaves at 6'.
'The train' is like 'it', so the verb needs -s: 'leaves'.
'Will leaving' is not a valid form. For a scheduled time, use the present simple: 'leaves'.

items.question: My parents ___ in Barcelona, but I live in Girona.
explanations: 'My parents' is plural (they), so no -s: 'live'. The -s is only for he/she/it.
Correct! Plural subject (they) takes the base verb: 'My parents live in Barcelona'.
'Are living' suggests a temporary situation. A permanent home takes the present simple: 'live'.
'Living' needs an auxiliary to be a verb. The plain fact takes 'live'.

items.question: How often ___ you ___ to the gym?
explanations: Correct! 'You' takes 'do', and after it the base verb: 'How often do you go?'.
After 'do' the verb is the base form: 'go', not 'goes'.
'Are … go' mixes structures. A present simple question uses do/does: 'do you go?'.
'Does' is for he/she/it, not 'you'. With 'you' use 'do'.

items.question: Rewrite with the same meaning: 'He isn't in the habit of eating breakfast.'
explanations: 'Isn't eating … now' is present continuous about this moment, not a habit.
Correct! A habit he does not have = present simple negative with a frequency adverb: 'He doesn't usually eat breakfast'.
'Didn't eat' is past simple — one finished occasion, not a general habit.
'Won't eat' is about the future, not a present habit.
